title: Failed payments on completed orders
description: Return user_id, name, order_id, payment_method, and amount for orders that are marked as completed but have a payment status of either 'failed' or 'chargeback'. Order by amount descending, then order_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, o.order_id, p.payment_method, p.amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status IN ('failed', 'chargeback')
ORDER BY p.amount DESC, o.order_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, o.order_id, p.payment_method, p.amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status IN ('failed', 'chargeback')
ORDER BY p.amount DESC, o.order_id ASC;
explanation: This query joins users to their orders, then joins those orders to payments so each returned row has both customer and payment context. The two WHERE conditions are important because order status and payment status describe different stages of the transaction lifecycle. Filtering after the joins ensures only completed orders with failed or chargeback payments are returned. The ORDER BY clause makes the output deterministic and highlights the largest problematic payments first.
hints: Combine the sources that hold the needed attributes before you filter or shape the final result.
Filter on both the order status and the payment status.
complexity_hint: O(n) scan with early filter pushdown. The two WHERE conditions reduce the result before sorting.

title: Products in refunded orders
description: Return each distinct product_name and brand for products that appear in orders whose payment_status is 'refunded'. Order by product_name ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_name, p.brand
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE o.payment_status = 'refunded'
ORDER BY p.product_name ASC
solution_query:
SELECT DISTINCT p.product_name, p.brand
FROM orders o
JOIN order_items oi ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE o.payment_status = 'refunded'
ORDER BY p.product_name ASC;
explanation: The query first links refunded orders to their order items, then links those line items to products so product details can be returned. DISTINCT is necessary because the same product can appear in multiple refunded orders, and the question asks for unique products rather than one row per order item. The WHERE clause applies the business filter at the order level, while ORDER BY makes the final list deterministic and easy to review.
hints: Combine the sources that hold the needed attributes before you filter or shape the final result.
Use DISTINCT because the same product may appear in more than one refunded order.

title: Departments without employees
description: Return department_id, department_name, and region for departments that do not have any employees assigned to them. Order by department_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
WHERE e.employee_id IS NULL
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
WHERE e.employee_id IS NULL
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC;
explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps every department even when there is no matching employee row. After the join, departments with no employees will have NULL values from the employees table, so filtering on e.employee_id IS NULL isolates exactly those unmatched departments. This is a standard medium-level join pattern because it depends on understanding how NULLs behave after an outer join. ORDER BY department_id keeps the result deterministic.
hints: Keep the full reference list in view, then identify which entries never found a match on the related side.
After the join, look for NULL on a column from the employees table.
complexity_hint: LEFT JOIN anti-pattern: keep rows from the left table that have no match on the right. Can also be written with NOT IN or NOT EXISTS — LEFT JOIN + IS NULL is typically most performant.

alternative_solution.query:
SELECT d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
FROM departments d
WHERE d.department_id NOT IN (
  SELECT department_id FROM employees
)
ORDER BY d.department_id ASC
alternative_solution.explanation: This alternative uses a NOT IN subquery instead of LEFT JOIN + IS NULL. Both produce identical results here, but NOT IN can silently return no rows if the subquery produces any NULLs — use LEFT JOIN + IS NULL in production for safety.

title: Paid revenue by user country
description: Return each country and the total paid payment amount for completed orders from users in that country as total_paid_amount. Order by total_paid_amount descending, then country ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, SUM(p.amount) AS total_paid_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY total_paid_amount DESC, u.country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, SUM(p.amount) AS total_paid_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
 AND p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.country
ORDER BY total_paid_amount DESC, u.country ASC;
explanation: This query requires joining before aggregating because the country lives on users, the order completion state lives on orders, and the payment amount lives on payments. The WHERE clause narrows the data to completed orders with paid payments before the SUM is calculated. GROUP BY country then rolls those filtered rows up to one revenue total per country. ORDER BY surfaces the highest-revenue countries first and guarantees deterministic output.
hints: Bring together the sources that hold the required attributes, then summarize at the business grain named in the prompt.
Filter first, then group by country and sum the payment amount.
complexity_hint: O(n log n) due to GROUP BY sort. Filtering with WHERE before aggregation is the key to keeping this efficient.

title: Country-plan segments with high average order value
description: Return country, plan_tier, and the average net_amount of completed orders as avg_completed_amount for country and plan tier combinations whose average completed order value is greater than 600. Order by avg_completed_amount descending, then country ascending, then plan_tier ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier, AVG(o.net_amount) AS avg_completed_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING AVG(o.net_amount) > 600
ORDER BY avg_completed_amount DESC, u.country ASC, u.plan_tier ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier, AVG(o.net_amount) AS avg_completed_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING AVG(o.net_amount) > 600
ORDER BY avg_completed_amount DESC, u.country ASC, u.plan_tier ASC;
explanation: The join links each order to user attributes so the aggregation can happen at the country and plan tier level. WHERE filters the dataset down to completed orders before grouping, which ensures cancelled orders do not affect the average. GROUP BY on two columns creates a segment-level view, and HAVING filters those aggregated segments after AVG has been computed. This is exactly the kind of medium reasoning where WHERE and HAVING must be used for different purposes.
hints: Bring together the sources that hold the required attributes, then summarize at the business grain named in the prompt.
Use HAVING, not WHERE, to keep only groups whose average net_amount is above 600.

title: Users with multiple paid payment methods
description: Return user_id, name, and the number of distinct payment methods used across paid payments as payment_method_count. Include only users who have used more than one distinct payment method. Order by payment_method_count descending, then user_id ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) AS payment_method_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) > 1
ORDER BY payment_method_count DESC, u.user_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) AS payment_method_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
WHERE p.status = 'paid'
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.payment_method) > 1
ORDER BY payment_method_count DESC, u.user_id ASC;
explanation: This query joins users to orders and then to payments so payment method behavior can be analyzed per user. COUNT(DISTINCT payment_method) is important because repeated use of the same method should count once, not once per payment row. WHERE limits the analysis to paid payments before the grouping happens. HAVING then filters the grouped results to only users who truly used more than one distinct payment method.
hints: Bring together the sources that hold the required attributes, then summarize at the business grain named in the prompt.
Use HAVING after GROUP BY to keep only users whose distinct payment method count is greater than 1.

title: Slow resolved ticket groups
description: Return priority, issue_type, and the average resolution_hours for resolved tickets as avg_resolution_hours. Include only priority and issue type groups whose average resolution_hours is greater than 72. Order by avg_resolution_hours descending, then priority ascending, then issue_type ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT priority, issue_type, AVG(resolution_hours) AS avg_resolution_hours
FROM support_tickets
WHERE status = 'resolved'
GROUP BY priority, issue_type
HAVING AVG(resolution_hours) > 72
ORDER BY avg_resolution_hours DESC, priority ASC, issue_type ASC
solution_query:
SELECT priority, issue_type, AVG(resolution_hours) AS avg_resolution_hours
FROM support_tickets
WHERE status = 'resolved'
GROUP BY priority, issue_type
HAVING AVG(resolution_hours) > 72
ORDER BY avg_resolution_hours DESC, priority ASC, issue_type ASC;
explanation: The WHERE clause limits the input rows to resolved tickets because unresolved tickets should not affect resolution-time analysis. GROUP BY priority and issue_type creates a separate aggregate for each operational category. HAVING then removes groups whose average resolution time is not high enough to be considered slow. This structure demonstrates the medium-level difference between filtering raw rows first and filtering aggregated groups later.
hints: Reduce the data to the requested segment first, then keep only the segments that clear the summary threshold.
Because the filter is on an aggregate, use HAVING rather than WHERE.

title: Order value bands
description: Bucket orders into three bands based on net_amount: 'low value' for amounts under 100, 'mid value' for amounts from 100 up to but not including 500, and 'high value' for amounts of 500 or more. Return order_value_band and order_count. Order by order_count descending, then order_value_band ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END AS order_value_band,
 COUNT(order_id) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END
ORDER BY order_count DESC, order_value_band ASC
solution_query:
SELECT CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END AS order_value_band,
 COUNT(order_id) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY CASE
 WHEN net_amount < 100 THEN 'low value'
 WHEN net_amount < 500 THEN 'mid value'
 ELSE 'high value'
 END
ORDER BY order_count DESC, order_value_band ASC;
explanation: CASE WHEN creates a derived label for each order based on its net_amount, which turns a continuous numeric value into business-friendly categories. Because the output is summarized by those labels, the same CASE expression must appear in the GROUP BY clause. COUNT then measures how many orders fall into each band. The ORDER BY clause makes the summary deterministic and shows the most common band first.
hints: Collapse the data to the business grain named in the result before you calculate the summary values.
Group by the same CASE expression you use in the SELECT list.

title: Salary bands by department region

expected_query:

 CASE WHEN e.salary >= 100000 THEN 'high salary' ELSE 'standard salary' END AS salary_band,
 COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
GROUP BY d.region,
 CASE WHEN e.salary >= 100000 THEN 'high salary' ELSE 'standard salary' END
ORDER BY d.region ASC, salary_band ASC
solution_query:

 CASE WHEN e.salary >= 100000 THEN 'high salary' ELSE 'standard salary' END AS salary_band,

title: Employees above their department average

expected_query:
SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name, d.department_name, e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE e.salary > (
 SELECT AVG(e2.salary)
 FROM employees e2
 WHERE e2.department_id = e.department_id
)
ORDER BY d.department_name ASC, e.salary DESC, e.employee_id ASC
solution_query:
SELECT e.employee_id, e.employee_name, d.department_name, e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE e.salary > (
 SELECT AVG(e2.salary)
 FROM employees e2
 WHERE e2.department_id = e.department_id
)
ORDER BY d.department_name ASC, e.salary DESC, e.employee_id ASC;
explanation: The outer query returns employee details along with the department name. The correlated subquery recalculates the average salary for the current employee's department by matching e2.department_id to e.department_id. Because that subquery depends on the outer row, it produces a department-specific comparison instead of one global average. The WHERE clause keeps only employees whose salary is above their own department average, and the ORDER BY produces a stable result.

title: Country order outcome mix
description: Return country, completed_orders, and cancelled_orders for countries with at least 50 total orders. Count orders from the orders table joined to users. Order by completed_orders descending, then country ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.country,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_orders,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_orders
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.country
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 50
ORDER BY completed_orders DESC, u.country ASC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'completed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS completed_orders,
 SUM(CASE WHEN o.status = 'cancelled' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cancelled_orders
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
GROUP BY u.country
HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 50
ORDER BY completed_orders DESC, u.country ASC;
explanation: This query uses conditional aggregation to produce two different counts from the same grouped set of order rows. After joining orders to users, rows are grouped by country. Each SUM(CASE WHEN ...) counts only the rows that match one status condition, while HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) >= 50 removes countries with too little activity. This pattern is useful when several related metrics need to be computed in a single grouped query.
hints: Bring together the sources that hold the required attributes, then summarize at the business grain named in the prompt.
Use CASE inside an aggregate to count only rows with one status at a time.

title: Ticket resolution mix by issue type
description: Return issue_type, resolved_ticket_count, unresolved_ticket_count, and avg_resolved_hours. unresolved_ticket_count should include tickets whose status is 'open' or 'in_progress'. avg_resolved_hours should average resolution_hours only for resolved tickets. Include only issue types with at least 20 total tickets. Order by resolved_ticket_count descending, then issue_type ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT issue_type,
 SUM(CASE WHEN status = 'resolved' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS resolved_ticket_count,
 SUM(CASE WHEN status IN ('open', 'in_progress') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS unresolved_ticket_count,

title: Quarter-over-quarter revenue change
description:
Compute total `net_amount` per calendar quarter from the `orders` table. Label each quarter as `'YYYY-QN'` (e.g. `'2024-Q1'`). Use LAG() to compute `qoq_change` = current quarter revenue minus the previous quarter. Return `quarter`, `total_revenue` (rounded to 2 decimal places), and `qoq_change` (rounded to 2 decimal places; NULL for the first quarter). Order by `quarter` ascending.

To derive the quarter number: `((CAST(STRFTIME('%m', order_date) AS INT) - 1) / 3 + 1)`.
expected_query:
WITH quarterly AS (
    SELECT STRFTIME('%Y', order_date) || '-Q' ||
           CAST(((CAST(STRFTIME('%m', order_date) AS INT) - 1) / 3 + 1) AS TEXT) AS quarter,
           ROUND(SUM(net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY quarter
)
SELECT quarter,
       total_revenue,
       ROUND(total_revenue - LAG(total_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY quarter), 2) AS qoq_change
FROM quarterly
ORDER BY quarter
solution_query:
WITH quarterly AS (
    SELECT STRFTIME('%Y', order_date) || '-Q' ||
           CAST(((CAST(STRFTIME('%m', order_date) AS INT) - 1) / 3 + 1) AS TEXT) AS quarter,
           ROUND(SUM(net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
    FROM orders
    GROUP BY quarter
)
SELECT quarter,
       total_revenue,
       ROUND(total_revenue - LAG(total_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY quarter), 2) AS qoq_change
FROM quarterly
ORDER BY quarter;
explanation: A CTE first aggregates revenue per quarter. The quarter label is built by concatenating the year (STRFTIME('%Y')), '-Q', and the quarter number derived from the month. In the outer query, LAG(total_revenue) OVER (ORDER BY quarter) fetches the previous row's revenue — because quarters sort lexicographically in 'YYYY-QN' format, this gives the correct prior quarter. Subtracting gives qoq_change; the first row has no prior, so LAG returns NULL.
hints: Create row-to-row context within each entity first; the final filter or comparison happens after that derived state exists.
LAG(col) OVER (ORDER BY quarter) gives you the previous row's value — subtract it from the current row to get the change

title: Full outer join: order-payment reconciliation
description:
Identify mismatches between orders and payments. Perform a FULL OUTER JOIN between `orders` (alias `o`) and `payments` (alias `p`) on `order_id`. Return:
- `order_id`: `COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id)`
- `order_amount`: `o.net_amount` (NULL if no order record)
- `payment_amount`: `p.amount` (NULL if no payment record)
- `reconciliation_status`: `'order_only'` if payment is NULL, `'payment_only'` if order is NULL, `'matched'` if both exist

Order by `order_id` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) AS order_id,
       o.net_amount AS order_amount,
       p.amount AS payment_amount,
       CASE
           WHEN p.order_id IS NULL THEN 'order_only'
           WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN 'payment_only'
           ELSE 'matched'
       END AS reconciliation_status
FROM orders o
FULL OUTER JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
ORDER BY order_id
solution_query:
SELECT COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) AS order_id,
       o.net_amount AS order_amount,
       p.amount AS payment_amount,
       CASE
           WHEN p.order_id IS NULL THEN 'order_only'
           WHEN o.order_id IS NULL THEN 'payment_only'
           ELSE 'matched'
       END AS reconciliation_status
FROM orders o
FULL OUTER JOIN payments p ON o.order_id = p.order_id
ORDER BY order_id;
explanation: FULL OUTER JOIN returns all rows from both tables, filling NULL for columns from the table with no match. COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) produces a non-NULL order_id regardless of which side has the match. The CASE expression classifies each row: when p.order_id is NULL, only an order exists; when o.order_id is NULL, only a payment exists; otherwise both match. This reconciliation pattern is essential in financial data pipelines to detect payment gaps or orphaned transactions.
hints: Think about reconciliation rather than matching only successes; the answer must preserve records from both sides, including misses.
Use COALESCE(o.order_id, p.order_id) to get a non-null order_id regardless of which side matched

FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
ORDER BY employee_count DESC, d.department_name ASC

       COUNT(e.employee_id) AS employee_count
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name, d.region
ORDER BY employee_count DESC, d.department_name ASC;
explanation: The JOIN direction matters: LEFT JOIN from departments to employees preserves all department rows even when no employees exist. COUNT(e.employee_id) counts only non-NULL employee IDs — for departments with no employees, this returns 0 (not 1 or NULL). Using COUNT(*) instead would incorrectly count 1 for empty departments because the LEFT JOIN produces a row with NULLs. Grouping by department_id ensures uniqueness even if two departments share the same name.

FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING SUM(o.net_amount) > 5000
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
solution_query:
SELECT u.country, u.plan_tier,
       ROUND(SUM(o.net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
FROM orders o
JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.country, u.plan_tier
HAVING SUM(o.net_amount) > 5000
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
explanation: WHERE filters rows before aggregation (only completed orders contribute). GROUP BY produces one row per (country, plan_tier) combination. HAVING filters groups after aggregation — it's the only way to filter on aggregate values like SUM. Using WHERE SUM(...) > 5000 would be a syntax error. The distinction between WHERE (row-level filter) and HAVING (group-level filter) is one of the most commonly tested SQL concepts.

title: Above-average spenders
description: Identify users whose total net amount from completed orders exceeds the average total spend across all users who placed at least one completed order. Return `user_id`, `name`, `country`, and `total_spend` (rounded to 2 decimal places), ordered by `total_spend` descending.

solution_query:

title: Payment failure rate by method
description: For each payment method, compute the number of successful (`paid`) payments, the number of `failed` payments, and the failure rate as a percentage. Return `payment_method`, `paid_count`, `failed_count`, and `failure_rate_pct` (rounded to 1 decimal place, calculated as failed count / total count × 100), ordered by `failure_rate_pct` descending.
expected_query:
SELECT payment_method,
       COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'paid' THEN 1 END) AS paid_count,
       COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'failed' THEN 1 END) AS failed_count,
       ROUND(COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'failed' THEN 1 END) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 1) AS failure_rate_pct
FROM payments
GROUP BY payment_method
ORDER BY failure_rate_pct DESC
solution_query:
SELECT payment_method,
       COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'paid' THEN 1 END) AS paid_count,
       COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'failed' THEN 1 END) AS failed_count,
       ROUND(COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'failed' THEN 1 END) * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 1) AS failure_rate_pct
FROM payments
GROUP BY payment_method
ORDER BY failure_rate_pct DESC;
explanation: Conditional aggregation with CASE WHEN counts specific status values without needing subqueries. COUNT(CASE WHEN ... THEN 1 END) returns NULL for non-matching rows, so only matches are counted. Multiplying by 100.0 forces float division. COUNT(*) in the denominator includes all statuses to compute the correct rate.
hints: Use CASE WHEN inside COUNT to count only rows matching a condition.

title: Category revenue share
description: For each product category, compute the total line revenue and its percentage share of total revenue across all categories. Return `category_name`, `total_revenue` (rounded to 2 decimal places), and `revenue_pct` (rounded to 1 decimal place), ordered by `total_revenue` descending.
expected_query:
WITH cat_revenue AS (
    SELECT c.category_name, ROUND(SUM(oi.line_amount),2) AS total_revenue
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
    JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
    GROUP BY c.category_name
),
total AS (SELECT SUM(total_revenue) AS grand_total FROM cat_revenue)
SELECT cr.category_name, cr.total_revenue,
       ROUND(cr.total_revenue * 100.0 / t.grand_total, 1) AS revenue_pct
FROM cat_revenue cr
CROSS JOIN total t
ORDER BY cr.total_revenue DESC
solution_query:
WITH cat_revenue AS (
    SELECT c.category_name, ROUND(SUM(oi.line_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
    JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
    GROUP BY c.category_name
),
total AS (SELECT SUM(total_revenue) AS grand_total FROM cat_revenue)
SELECT cr.category_name, cr.total_revenue,
       ROUND(cr.total_revenue * 100.0 / t.grand_total, 1) AS revenue_pct
FROM cat_revenue cr
CROSS JOIN total t
ORDER BY cr.total_revenue DESC;
explanation: Two CTEs chain the calculation: cat_revenue aggregates per-category revenue; total sums across all categories. CROSS JOIN with the single-row total CTE makes grand_total available for the percentage calculation. Multiplying by 100.0 forces float division.

CROSS JOIN with the single-row total CTE makes grand_total available in every result row.

Include only rows where `total_revenue` exceeds 50 000. Order by `month` ascending, then `total_revenue` descending.
expected_query:
SELECT u.acquisition_channel, strftime(o.order_date, '%Y-%m') AS month,
       ROUND(SUM(o.net_amount),2) AS total_revenue, COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.acquisition_channel, month
HAVING SUM(o.net_amount) > 50000
ORDER BY month ASC, total_revenue DESC
solution_query:
SELECT u.acquisition_channel,
       strftime(o.order_date, '%Y-%m') AS month,
       ROUND(SUM(o.net_amount), 2) AS total_revenue,
       COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
WHERE o.status = 'completed'
GROUP BY u.acquisition_channel, month
HAVING SUM(o.net_amount) > 50000
ORDER BY month ASC, total_revenue DESC;
explanation: strftime(order_date, '%Y-%m') extracts the year-month string for grouping. GROUP BY includes both acquisition_channel and the computed month alias. HAVING filters after aggregation — only channel+month combos with > 50 000 revenue appear. ORDER BY month ASC sorts chronologically; secondary total_revenue DESC ranks channels within each month.
hints: Use strftime to extract the year-month string from order_date.
HAVING filters on aggregate results — WHERE can't reference SUM(net_amount).

title: Top-spender loyalty tier segmentation
description: Compute each user's total spend, order count, and average order value from completed orders. Assign a `loyalty_tier`: `'Platinum'` if total spend > 20 000, `'Gold'` if > 10 000, otherwise `'Silver'`. Return only users whose total spend is at or above the 80th percentile. Columns: `user_id`, `name`, `country`, `total_spend`, `order_count`, `avg_order_value` (both rounded to 2 dp), and `loyalty_tier`. Order by `total_spend` descending.
expected_query:
WITH user_spend AS (
    SELECT u.user_id, u.name, u.country,
           ROUND(SUM(o.net_amount),2) AS total_spend,
           COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
           ROUND(AVG(o.net_amount),2) AS avg_order_value
    FROM users u
    JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
    WHERE o.status = 'completed'
    GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name, u.country
),
percentile AS (
    SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.80) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_spend) AS p80
    FROM user_spend
)
SELECT us.user_id, us.name, us.country, us.total_spend, us.order_count, us.avg_order_value,
       CASE WHEN us.total_spend > 20000 THEN 'Platinum'
            WHEN us.total_spend > 10000 THEN 'Gold'
            ELSE 'Silver'
       END AS loyalty_tier
FROM user_spend us
CROSS JOIN percentile p
WHERE us.total_spend >= p.p80
ORDER BY us.total_spend DESC
solution_query:
WITH user_spend AS (
    SELECT u.user_id, u.name, u.country,
           ROUND(SUM(o.net_amount), 2) AS total_spend,
           COUNT(o.order_id) AS order_count,
           ROUND(AVG(o.net_amount), 2) AS avg_order_value
    FROM users u
    JOIN orders o ON u.user_id = o.user_id
    WHERE o.status = 'completed'
    GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name, u.country
),
percentile AS (
    SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.80) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY total_spend) AS p80
    FROM user_spend
)
SELECT us.user_id, us.name, us.country, us.total_spend,
       us.order_count, us.avg_order_value,
       CASE WHEN us.total_spend > 20000 THEN 'Platinum'
            WHEN us.total_spend > 10000 THEN 'Gold'
            ELSE 'Silver'
       END AS loyalty_tier
FROM user_spend us
CROSS JOIN percentile p
WHERE us.total_spend >= p.p80
ORDER BY us.total_spend DESC;
explanation: user_spend CTE aggregates per-user metrics. percentile CTE uses PERCENTILE_CONT(0.80) WITHIN GROUP to compute the 80th-percentile threshold. CROSS JOIN with the single-row percentile CTE makes p80 available in WHERE. CASE WHEN evaluates loyalty thresholds in descending order (most exclusive first).
hints: Computing a percentile threshold requires a special ordered-set aggregate function — think about which SQL function handles percentiles.
CROSS JOIN the single-row percentile CTE to make p80 available in the WHERE filter.

title: Category revenue ranking with parent and gap
description: For each product category compute `total_revenue` (rounded to 2 dp), `revenue_rank` (1 = highest, using `RANK()`), `parent_category`, and `revenue_vs_top` — how far each category's revenue is below the top category (positive number, rounded to 2 dp). Order by `revenue_rank` ascending.
expected_query:
WITH cat_revenue AS (
    SELECT c.category_name, c.category_id, ROUND(SUM(oi.line_amount),2) AS total_revenue
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
    JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
    GROUP BY c.category_name, c.category_id
)
SELECT cr.category_name, cr.total_revenue,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY cr.total_revenue DESC) AS revenue_rank,
       c.parent_category,
       ROUND(MAX(cr.total_revenue) OVER () - cr.total_revenue, 2) AS revenue_vs_top
FROM cat_revenue cr
JOIN categories c ON cr.category_id = c.category_id
ORDER BY revenue_rank
solution_query:
WITH cat_revenue AS (
    SELECT c.category_name, c.category_id,
           ROUND(SUM(oi.line_amount), 2) AS total_revenue
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
    JOIN categories c ON p.category_id = c.category_id
    GROUP BY c.category_name, c.category_id
)
SELECT cr.category_name, cr.total_revenue,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY cr.total_revenue DESC) AS revenue_rank,
       c.parent_category,
       ROUND(MAX(cr.total_revenue) OVER () - cr.total_revenue, 2) AS revenue_vs_top
FROM cat_revenue cr
JOIN categories c ON cr.category_id = c.category_id
ORDER BY revenue_rank;
explanation: The CTE computes per-category revenue. RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_revenue DESC) assigns global rank. MAX(total_revenue) OVER () — with no PARTITION BY — returns the single highest revenue across all rows, enabling the gap calculation. A second JOIN to categories fetches parent_category, which wasn't needed during aggregation.
hints: Assign each category a position in the global revenue ranking before computing the gap to the top.
MAX(...) OVER () with no PARTITION BY returns the maximum across all result rows — use it to compute the gap to the top.

title: Department salary rank and above-average count
description: For each department, compute the headcount, average salary (rounded to 2 dp), salary rank across all departments (1 = highest average salary, using `RANK()`), and the count of employees whose individual salary exceeds the company-wide average. Return `department_name`, `headcount`, `avg_salary`, `salary_rank`, and `above_avg_count`, ordered by `salary_rank` ascending.
expected_query:
SELECT d.department_name, COUNT(e.employee_id) AS headcount,
       ROUND(AVG(e.salary),2) AS avg_salary,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY AVG(e.salary) DESC) AS salary_rank,
       COUNT(CASE WHEN e.salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) THEN 1 END) AS above_avg_count
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_name, d.department_id
ORDER BY salary_rank
solution_query:
SELECT d.department_name,
       COUNT(e.employee_id) AS headcount,
       ROUND(AVG(e.salary), 2) AS avg_salary,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY AVG(e.salary) DESC) AS salary_rank,
       COUNT(CASE WHEN e.salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) THEN 1 END) AS above_avg_count
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.department_id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_name, d.department_id
ORDER BY salary_rank;
explanation: RANK() OVER (ORDER BY AVG(e.salary) DESC) applies a window function to the aggregated value — valid after GROUP BY. The scalar subquery (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) computes the company-wide average and is used inside CASE WHEN for the above_avg_count. GROUP BY must include department_id to ensure unique grouping.
hints: A position/ordering function can be applied to the per-department averages that aggregation produces.
Use a scalar subquery to compute the company-wide average salary inside CASE WHEN.
